fuzzy_prd: Our build system needs to work with software release identifiers — the kind that have a major number, optional minor and patch, optional pre-release label, and optional build stamp. Right now every tool team re-derives the same comparison and range logic. We need a shared library that can: (1) parse and normalize a version string so we know it's valid and have a canonical form; (2) compare two versions and tell us which comes first; (3) take a version range expression — including the shorthand formats like 'at least 1.2', 'compatible with 1.2.3', 'between these two versions', and the version shorthand used in our dependency manifests — and normalize it to a canonical comparable form; and (4) test whether a concrete version falls inside a given range. The tricky cases are pre-release labels (they should rank below the corresponding release), wildcards (both explicit and implicit), and the two shorthand range styles that follow the conventions in standard package-manager ecosystems. Edge case to handle: a range expressed as just a major version like '1' should expand the same way our package manifest tooling does — look at how that expansion is done in the existing version utilities to stay consistent.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with action) -> stdout semantic-version parse/compare/range lines

trigger_keywords: what does an empty range string expand to
how is a wildcard-only range canonicalized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An empty string or a bare wildcard ('*', 'x', 'X') canonicalizes to the single token '*', which matches every version.

trigger_keywords: how does a partial version expand in a range
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A partial version like '1' (major only) expands to '>=1.0.0-0 <2.0.0-0'. The synthesized bounds include a '-0' pre-release suffix so that pre-release versions at the boundary are handled correctly.

trigger_keywords: do pre-release versions satisfy a non-prerelease bound
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A pre-release version satisfies a comparator only when the comparator itself targets a pre-release at the same major.minor.patch triple. '>4.8' expands to '>=4.9.0-0', which has a pre-release '-0' at 4.9.0, so '4.9.0-beta' (which is > 4.9.0-0) is accepted. But '<4.9' expands to '<4.9.0-0', so '4.9.0-beta' is rejected.

For caret ranges, when major is non-zero the major is the leftmost non-zero component and is held fixed (e.g. '^1.2.3' → '>=1.2.3 <2.0.0-0'). When major is 0, the first non-zero component (minor if non-zero, otherwise patch) is held fixed. '^0' → '>=0.0.0 <1.0.0'.

trigger_keywords: output line format for compare action
what three lines does compare print
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The compare action prints exactly three lines: 'left=<canonical>
', 'right=<canonical>
', 'order=<word>
'. The order word is one of: 'less', 'equal', or 'greater', describing the placement of 'left' relative to 'right'.

Full semver engine surface: parse versions with prerelease/build metadata, compare versions including prerelease precedence, canonicalize ranges including exact, primitive comparators, AND/OR conjunctions, hyphen ranges, tilde ranges, caret/wildcard/shorthand ranges, empty ranges, invalid version/range errors, and test_range satisfaction behavior.
Route by action values parse, compare, canonicalize_range, and test_range. Preserve TypeScript semver canonical rendering and boolean satisfaction outputs from public_test_cases.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the expansion done in the existing version utilities
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The semver package at internal/semver/ in the TypeScript-Go repository, particularly TryParseVersionRange which performs the canonical expansion of partial/wildcard/tilde/caret ranges.

context_pointers.vague_reference: shorthand formats like 'compatible with' and the ones used in dependency manifests
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Tilde ranges (~) and caret ranges (^), as well as hyphen ranges (A - B) and disjunctions (||) — all standard npm/semver range syntaxes.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Consult the internal/semver package in the Go source for exact parsing and matching behavior. The dispatcher at rcb_tests/dispatcher/main.go shows the action-to-function mapping.

feature1_parse_version.json.description: Parse a textual version identifier and emit its canonical normalized form. The identifier has up to three dot-separated numeric components plus an optional hyphen-introduced pre-release segment and an optional plus-introduced build-metadata segment. The adapter parses the identifier and prints back the canonical rendering of the parsed value, so a well-formed identifier round-trips to itself.

feature2_compare_versions.json.description: Compare two version identifiers for precedence and report their relative ordering. Numeric major, minor and patch components are compared first; a pre-release segment lowers precedence relative to the otherwise-equal release; pre-release identifiers are compared field by field with numeric fields ordered numerically and alphanumeric fields ordered lexically; build metadata does not affect precedence. The adapter prints both operands in canonical form and a word naming the ordering of the first relative to the second.

feature3_canonicalize_range.json.description: Parse a version-range expression and emit its canonical normalized form. A range may use omitted trailing components or explicit wildcard placeholders to mean 'any value' for a position. The adapter expands such a range into the equivalent bounded form, so that every spelling denoting the same set of versions produces an identical canonical rendering; an unrestricted range renders as the universal-match token.

feature4_1_primitive_ranges.json.description: Decide whether a version satisfies a range built from a single primitive comparator or a partial/wildcard version. Supported forms include an unrestricted range, an exact match, an explicit equality, and strictly-less, less-or-equal, strictly-greater, and greater-or-equal bounds, each possibly using a partial (wildcard) operand. Pre-release versions only satisfy a bound when that bound itself targets a pre-release at the same release, reflecting that a pre-release ranks below its release. The adapter prints the canonical range, the canonical version, and whether the version is contained.

feature4_2_conjunction_ranges.json.description: Decide whether a version satisfies a conjunction of comparators written side by side and separated by spaces. A version is contained only when it independently satisfies every comparator in the conjunction. The adapter prints the canonical range, the canonical version, and whether the version is contained.

feature4_3_disjunction_ranges.json.description: Decide whether a version satisfies a disjunction of alternatives separated by a double-pipe token. Each alternative is itself a conjunction of comparators; the version is contained when it satisfies at least one alternative. The adapter prints the canonical range, the canonical version, and whether the version is contained.

feature4_4_hyphen_ranges.json.description: Decide whether a version satisfies an inclusive interval written as a lower bound, a spaced hyphen, and an upper bound. Both endpoints are inclusive, so a version is contained when it is at least the lower bound and at most the upper bound. The adapter prints the canonical range, the canonical version, and whether the version is contained.

feature4_5_tilde_ranges.json.description: Decide whether a version satisfies a tilde range, which permits changes only in the least significant component that was specified. When a minor component is given, only patch-level increases are allowed; when only a major component is given, minor-level increases within that major are allowed. The adapter prints the canonical range, the canonical version, and whether the version is contained.

feature4_6_caret_ranges.json.description: Decide whether a version satisfies a caret range, which permits changes that do not modify the leftmost non-zero component. For a release whose major is non-zero, any higher minor or patch within the same major is allowed; when the major is zero, the first non-zero component (minor, else patch) is the one held fixed. The adapter prints the canonical range, the canonical version, and whether the version is contained.
